preamble: The shift from the voiced velar stop [g] to the voiced bilabial stop [b] typically occurs when the original sound is a labialized velar [gʷ] (produced with lip rounding). If a language loses the velar closure but retains the lip rounding, the sound 'slides' forward to become a pure bilabial stop. This is a classic example of labialization.

examples.note: PIE labiovelar *gʷ → Greek [b] before back vowels. Cf. Latin 'bos' showing the same shift.
Same PIE *gʷ → Greek [b] before front vowels.
Latin /gw/ cluster → Romanian [b] in Balkan Romance; the velar element was lost and the labial retained.
P-Celtic/Brythonic: PIE labiovelars systematically shifted to labials, distinguishing P-Celtic from Q-Celtic (which has [k]).

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction. MIT Press.
